spx.summary:
## S&P structure specialist — train-sandbox findings (2014-01-02 → 2025-07-10, 2,897 days)

All numbers measured on `~/projects/alpha-lab/research/hunt2026/train.parquet` under the harness convention (weights at close t earn t+1 close-to-close; costs 10bps/side stocks, 2bps ETFs on |Δw|). Baselines: SPY 1x CAGR 13.2% (Sh 0.80), QQQ 1x CAGR 18.4% (Sh 0.89), SPY 2x const CAGR 24.3% (Sh 0.80, MDD huge).

### Effects that are DEAD in train (do not build on these)
- **Index add/drop effect**: 265 adds / 259 drops from member-mask flips. Post-add excess vs SPY: −0.5% over 21d (t=−0.79); deletion reversal 0-63d: +0.27% (t=0.10). The classic index effect is gone in this sample — consistent with post-2010 literature.
- **Overnight-share tilt**: overnight carries 9.0%/yr of the 13.1%/yr eq-wt member return vs 4.1% intraday, but a 126d overnight-minus-intraday tilt has ZERO cross-sectional predictive power for c2c returns (Q5−Q1 = −0.6%/yr, t=−0.15). The overnight premium is real but not exploitable under a close-to-close convention.
- **12-1 momentum within S&P500**: decile spread +1.5%/yr, t=0.18. Flat.
- **ETF dual-momentum rotation** (best of SPY/QQQ/IWM/TLT/GLD, 6m lookback, monthly): 1.4% CAGR on train. Whipsawed to death 2015/2018/2020/2022.
- **Cross-sectional short-term reversal, net**: gross is real (5d sector-relative reversal decile spread +15.8%/yr, t=2.41; loser decile 20.1%/yr gross) but 10bps/side kills it: daily-rebalanced long loser-quintile turns 73%/day → net −1.1%/yr; EMA-smoothed (hl=5) best case nets 12.3% CAGR — below SPY. Market-neutral z-weighted version nets −2% to −23%. Reversal conditioning on high VIX / high dispersion doubles the gross spread (+27.8%/yr in VIX>80th-pct regimes) but n is small (t≈1.1) and costs still eat it.
- **Sep weakness** (−21.8% ann, t=−1.35) — not significant. Nov/Jul strength (t≈3.3) is sample-specific.

### Effects that SURVIVE costs (all at the 2bps ETF tier)
1. **Volatility-managed leverage (Moreira–Muir)** — w = min(2, σ_target/realized 21d vol). On SPY tgt 25%: CAGR 21.4%, Sh 0.95, MDD −30% (vs 0.80 unmanaged). On QQQ tgt 25%: **CAGR 25.4%, Sh 1.02, MDD −35%**. Robust: tgt 0.20/0.25/0.30 → 21.4/25.4/29.4% CAGR, vol lookback 10/21/42d → 20.3/21.4/18.7% (SPY), and positive in ALL three subperiods (2014-17, 2018-21, 2022-25: QQQ tgt25 gives 27/31/17%). Turnover ~2-4%/day → cost <0.2%/yr. This is the single most robust structure found.
2. **VIX-regime SVXY carry**: SVXY buy&hold CAGR is −3.5% (Feb-2018 Volmageddon −90%). Gated to days when VIX < its 63d median: CAGR 17.5%, Sh 0.77, MDD −31%, at only 0.53 avg exposure — the filter sidesteps the blowup. Threshold sensitivity q=0.4/0.5/0.6 → 14.5/17.5/20.8%. Caveat: SVXY deleveraged to −0.5x after Feb 2018; post-2018 subperiods run 11-12%/yr, so the sleeve is additive, not a standalone.
3. **Breadth-gated leverage**: 2x SPY when RSP/SPY 63d relative return > 0 (broad participation), else vol-managed at 15%: CAGR 22.7%, Sh 0.96. BUT lookback-fragile (42d → 17.7%, 126d → 17.0%) and 2022-25 subperiod only 9%. Treat as a stretch.
4. **Turn-of-month**: last-4+first-3 days carry ~20%/yr vs ~10% on other days (t≈2.2), but a 2x-TOM overlay gives CAGR 17.3% at Sh 0.78 — no Sharpe improvement over constant leverage. Real but too small to be a pillar.

### Combined book (best train result)
60% vol-managed QQQ (tgt 0.25) + 40% vol-managed SPY (tgt 0.25) + 0.3x SVXY when VIX<63d median, gross scaled to ≤2.0: **CAGR 29.1%, Sharpe 1.07, MDD −34%; subperiods 36/31/20%**. Honesty check: rolling 12m return is below +18% in 33% of windows and the worst 12m is −30.7%. The bar is cleared in expectation, not with certainty — the 18% depends on the equity premium showing up in the holdout year.

candidates.name: Vol-managed levered QQQ (Moreira-Muir vol timing)
candidates.mechanism: Realized vol clusters but does not forecast returns, so return-per-unit-risk is highest when vol is low. The other side is leverage-constrained and drawdown-averse investors (and vol-target mandates that de-risk AFTER vol spikes, at the lows) who cannot or will not hold 1.5-2x exposure in calm markets and who sell into high-vol episodes. The strategy harvests the equity premium at near-constant risk: levered in calm regimes, mechanically small through crashes. The edge persists because it requires leverage, daily discipline, and tolerance for lagging in V-shaped recoveries.
candidates.construction: At each close t: rv = std(QQQ close-to-close returns, last 21 days) * sqrt(252); target weight on QQQ = min(2.0, 0.25 / rv). All other tickers zero. Rebalance daily (turnover ~4%/day at 2bps ETF cost = ~0.2%/yr drag). Optional tolerance band: only trade when |w_new - w_current| > 0.05 to cut turnover further.
candidates.evidence: Train (2014-2025): CAGR 25.4%, Sharpe 1.02, MDD -35% net, vs QQQ 1x 18.4%/0.89. Positive and >15% in all three subperiods (2014-17: 27%, 2018-21: 31%, 2022-25H1: 17%). SPY version: 21.4%/0.95. Literature: Moreira & Muir (JF 2017) 'Volatility-Managed Portfolios' — vol-scaled factors raise Sharpe out-of-sample across factors and decades.
candidates.why_18pct: Arithmetic: avg exposure ~1.45x on an asset that returned 18.4%/yr on train, with de-risking clipping the worst days -> 25.4% net; costs are negligible at the ETF tier. Honest caveat: roughly 18 of those 25 points are levered QQQ beta from a strong tech decade — the vol timing adds ~2-4 points of CAGR and ~0.15 Sharpe, the rest is the equity premium. It clears +18% in any year where QQQ returns > ~10-12%; in a flat-to-down tech year it fails the bar (2022 standalone was deeply negative before the recovery). This is the most robust single design I found, but it is a levered-beta strategy with a genuine risk-timing kicker, not a market-neutral alpha.

candidates.name: Vol-managed QQQ+SPY core with VIX-gated SVXY carry sleeve
candidates.mechanism: Two premia, same buyer of insurance on the other side. Core: as above, harvesting equity premium at constant risk. Sleeve: the VIX futures curve is in contango most of the time because institutions systematically overpay for crash protection; short-VIX (SVXY) collects that insurance premium. The sleeve's fatal failure mode (short vol into a vol spike) is gated by only holding SVXY when VIX is below its own 63-day median — calm, contango-friendly regimes — which sidestepped Feb-2018 and Mar-2020 entirely on train.
candidates.construction: At each close t: w_QQQ = 0.6 * min(2, 0.25/rv21_QQQ); w_SPY = 0.4 * min(2, 0.25/rv21_SPY); w_SVXY = 0.3 if VIX_close < median(VIX close, last 63 days) else 0. If sum|w| > 2.0, scale all weights down proportionally (harness does this too). ^VIX is signal-only, never held. All ETF-tier costs.
params: svxy_weight = 0.3 when gate open (0.2-0.4 all fine)
vix_gate = 63d rolling median of VIX close (q=0.4-0.6 -> 14.5-20.8% standalone sleeve CAGR)
candidates.evidence: Train: CAGR 29.1%, Sharpe 1.07, MDD -34%, max gross 2.0, subperiods 36%/31%/20%. Sleeve standalone: 17.5% CAGR Sh 0.77 vs SVXY buy&hold -3.5% CAGR (gate avoids the -90% Volmageddon day). Contango carry premium: Eraker & Wu (2017), 'Explaining the Negative Returns to VIX Futures'.
candidates.why_18pct: Core alone nets 24.0% on train; sleeve adds ~5 points at +0.06 Sharpe. Even haircutting the tech-beta contribution hard (assume QQQ merely matches SPY at 13%), avg 1.5x exposure + ~3%/yr sleeve carry gives ~19-22% — the bar is met without needing 2014-2021 conditions to repeat exactly. Honest caveats: (1) rolling 12m return was below +18% in 33% of train windows, worst 12m -30.7% — this clears 18% in expectation, not with certainty; (2) SVXY halved its leverage post-2018 so pre-2018 sleeve numbers overstate it (post-2018 sleeve runs 11-12%/yr); (3) a holdout year containing a sustained bear fails the bar, gate or no gate. Best risk-adjusted package I can defend from train.

candidates.name: Breadth-gated leverage (RSP/SPY participation signal)
candidates.mechanism: When the equal-weight index (RSP) outruns the cap-weight index (SPY), the rally has broad participation — historically these regimes have higher forward Sharpe and fewer crashes, because narrow (mega-cap-only) rallies are late-cycle. The other side is benchmark-hugging investors who hold constant 1x exposure regardless of market internals. Breadth is public information but leverage-switching on it requires conviction and tolerance for whipsaw.
candidates.construction: At each close t: breadth = (RSP_t/SPY_t) / (RSP_{t-63}/SPY_{t-63}) - 1. If breadth > 0: w_SPY = 2.0. Else: w_SPY = min(2, 0.15/rv21_SPY). ETF-tier costs, turnover ~6%/day.
candidates.evidence: Train: CAGR 22.7%, Sharpe 0.96, MDD -39% at lb=63. Subperiods 20%/39%/9%. Breadth-conditional performance is a documented market-internals result (e.g., 'bad breadth' narrow-rally studies), but my 63d pick is the best of only three lookbacks tested.
candidates.why_18pct: STRETCH — flagging honestly. The 22.7% train CAGR clears the bar, but the signal is parameter-fragile (only the 63d lookback clears 18%; neighbors give ~17%) and the most recent subperiod (2022-2025H1) delivered just 9%/yr, which is exactly the regime most likely to resemble the holdout. Mega-cap-concentrated rallies (the 2023-2025 pattern) keep this strategy de-levered while SPY grinds up. I would only run it as a diversifier inside candidate 2's core, not standalone.

candidates.name: VIX-regime SVXY carry (standalone)
candidates.mechanism: Systematic overpricing of index crash insurance: VIX futures sit in contango ~80% of the time, so a short-VIX ETF earns roll yield. The buyers on the other side are hedgers with inelastic demand (mandated tail protection). The premium persists because the strategy has catastrophic left-tail risk that most capital will not warehouse — the edge IS the willingness to hold it, made survivable by regime-gating.
candidates.construction: At each close t: w_SVXY = 1.0 if VIX close < rolling 63d median of VIX close, else 0. Everything else zero. Avg exposure 0.53x, turnover ~11%/day at 2bps.
candidates.evidence: Train: CAGR 17.5%, Sharpe 0.77, MDD -31% — vs SVXY buy&hold CAGR -3.5% including the Feb-2018 -90% day, which the gate fully avoided (VIX was already above its 63d median for days before Volmageddon). Contango premium: Eraker & Wu (2017).
candidates.why_18pct: STRETCH standalone — 17.5% train CAGR is already just under the bar, and the honest number is worse: SVXY runs at -0.5x VIX futures since Feb 2018 (pre-2018 train years used the old -1x fund), and the post-2018 subperiods earned only 11-12%/yr. At 0.53x average exposure the carry simply is not big enough anymore. Levering the sleeve 2x when on would arithmetically clear 18% (2 x 11-12% post-2018) but doubles the tail. Belongs as the 0.3x sleeve inside candidate 2, not as a standalone book. Included because the gate's effect size (b&h -3.5% -> gated +17.5%) is the largest single conditioning effect I measured.

candidates.name: Turn-of-month levered overlay (rejected as pillar, viable as tilt)
candidates.mechanism: Month-end flows: pension/401k contributions, fund rebalancing, and window dressing concentrate buying pressure in the last ~4 and first ~3 trading days of each month. Calendar-day proxy only (no event data needed). The other side is liquidity providers who demand a premium for absorbing predictable flow.
candidates.construction: At each close t: base weight from candidate 1 (vol-managed QQQ); multiply by 1.25 on turn-of-month days (last 4 + first 3 trading days), scale to gross <= 2. Pure calendar rule, ETF-tier costs, turnover of the tilt ~10%/day on 7 days/month.
candidates.evidence: Train: TOM days carry ~20%/yr annualized vs ~10% on non-TOM days (t=2.18 for the TOM subset); last-2 and last-4 trading days strongest (t=2.13, 1.45). But a naive 2x-TOM/1x-rest SPY overlay gives CAGR 17.3% at Sharpe 0.78 — no Sharpe gain over constant leverage. Classic reference: Lakonishok & Smidt (1988) TOM effect.
candidates.why_18pct: FAILS standalone and I am saying so: the effect is statistically real in train (t~2.2) but economically small — the overlay version does not beat constant 2x SPY on either CAGR or Sharpe, because non-TOM days still carry ~half the premium and you give up exposure there. Worth ~0.5-1 point of CAGR as a tilt on candidate 1 at near-zero cost, worth nothing as a strategy. Listed so the orchestrator does not waste another agent's time rediscovering it.

firms.summary:
## Research summary: how small shops actually hit high-teens net on daily bars

Key takeaways from practitioner/academic sources, filtered for the sandbox (open/close/volume/membership + ~35 ETFs + spot ^VIX, daily close-to-close, 10bps/2bps, ≤2x gross):

**1. Unlevered daily-bar market-neutral does not get to 18%; documented paths that do are all (a) levered beta with a crash filter, (b) vol-regime timing of beta, or (c) concentrated momentum with leverage.** This matches how solo PMs and small CTAs actually run money — the "alpha" is mostly risk-timing on top of long beta.

**2. Trend-gated leverage is the single best-documented free-data path.** Gayed & Bilello's Dow-Award paper "Leverage for the Long Run" (SSRN 2741701): 2x S&P when above the 200d SMA, T-bills below, 1928-2015, beats buy-and-hold massively with ~5 trades/yr; the mechanism is that below-trend regimes carry the vol clusters and negative-compounding "volatility drag" that kill constant leverage. CXO Advisory and RealTest replications (setup4alpha) broadly confirm. This is regime-conditional beta, not alpha, but it is the most reliable +18% candidate the constraint set allows.

**3. Volatility-managed scaling works best exactly where we can apply it: momentum and equity beta.** Moreira & Muir (JF 2017, NBER w22208) show vol-scaling raises Sharpe; Cederburg et al. (JFE 2020) push back that most of the 103 strategies show no significant OOS gain — *except momentum-related ones*, where the gain is robust (consistent with Barroso & Santa-Clara 2015, Daniel & Moskowitz 2016). So: vol-scale the momentum book and the SPY book, don't pretend it upgrades everything.

**4. VIX-spike panic reversion is repeatedly documented and needs only spot ^VIX.** VIX>30 spike-and-recede entries show ~68-74% win rates and ~3x the unconditional 5-day SPY return (iPresage backtest; consistent with Nagel 2012's "evaporating liquidity" result that reversal/liquidity-provision profits concentrate when VIX is high). Usable as an episodic 2x overlay.

**5. Short-term reversal survives costs only in large caps with turnover discipline.** De Groot, Huij & Zhou ("Another Look at Trading Costs and Short-Term Reversal Profits", SSRN 1605049): 30-50 bps/WEEK net in large caps once you restrict the universe and slow turnover; Blitz et al. residual reversal doubles Sharpe by stripping factor moves. S&P500-only at 10bps/side is exactly their surviving configuration, but the premium has decayed post-2010 — standalone it's a stretch, as a sleeve it adds.

**6. Index-rebalance front-running is largely dead for this toolkit.** Greenwood & Sammon "The Disappearing Index Effect" (HBS WP 23-025): the addition pop has compressed toward zero and the trade is crowded; also we only see membership point-in-time (effective date), not announcements. Discarded. PEAD proper needs earnings dates, but a price/volume event proxy (big gap + volume spike) is freely derivable and keeps a drift edge at small size — included as the speculative sixth candidate.

**7. Sector/industry momentum via ETFs survives costs.** Moskowitz-Grinblatt industry momentum; ETF implementations documented at ~5%/yr excess after costs (Quantpedia "Sector Momentum — Rotational System"; QuantConnect dual-momentum sector rotation). At 2bps ETF costs and monthly turnover this is nearly cost-free.

**Honest bottom line:** none of these is 18%/yr of *alpha*. The candidates below clear +18% net mainly in years when the S&P is up mid-single-digits or better, by holding 1.5-2x smart beta and side-stepping the left tail. For the 12 months ending 2026-07-10 that is a defensible bet, not a certainty. Candidates 1, 2 and 5 are the credible clears; 3 is plausible; 4 and 6 are stretches included for diversification.

Sources: [Leverage for the Long Run (SSRN)](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2741701) · [CXO replication](https://www.cxoadvisory.com/volatility-effects/leveraging-the-u-s-stock-market-based-on-sma-rules/) · [RealTest replication](https://setup4alpha.substack.com/p/tested-award-winning-trading-strategy-realtest) · [Moreira & Muir, Volatility-Managed Portfolios](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2659431) · [Cederburg et al., On the performance of volatility-managed portfolios](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0304405X2030132X) · [de Groot, Huij & Zhou, trading costs and reversal](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/Delivery.cfm/SSRN_ID1894614_code340546.pdf?abstractid=1605049&mirid=1) · [Blitz et al., Short-Term Residual Reversal](https://www.efmaefm.org/0EFMSYMPOSIUM/2012/papers/017_update.pdf) · [Quantpedia, Short Term Reversal](https://quantpedia.com/strategies/short-term-reversal-in-stocks) · [Greenwood & Sammon, Disappearing Index Effect](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/23-025_563e45c6-df92-4d9c-ae05-608d4d0acab1.pdf) · [iPresage VIX mean reversion](https://www.ipresage.com/research/vix-mean-reversion) · [Quantpedia Sector Momentum](https://quantpedia.com/strategies/sector-momentum-rotational-system) · [QuantConnect dual momentum sector rotation](https://www.quantconnect.com/forum/discussion/527/dual-momentum-sector-rotation/p1) · [Quantpedia, index rebalance timing](https://quantpedia.com/should-we-rebalance-index-changes-immediately/)

candidates.name: Trend-Gated Levered SPY (Leverage-for-the-Long-Run)
candidates.mechanism: Volatility clusters and large negative days concentrate in below-trend regimes; constant leverage dies of volatility drag precisely there. Gating 2x exposure on an above-trend filter keeps the compounding math favorable while avoiding the states where levered beta self-destructs. The other side is every investor who de-risks slowly after drawdowns (institutional mandates, margin calls) and re-risks late; the edge persists because it costs its holder long flat/chop stretches of whipsaw underperformance that most allocators will not sit through.
candidates.construction: Signal: SPY close vs its N-day SMA of closes. If close > SMA(N): target weight 2.0 on SPY. If close < SMA(N): target weight 0 on SPY, 1.0 on a short-duration Treasury ETF from the panel (SHY/BIL; cash if absent). Add a hysteresis band: only switch when close crosses the SMA by more than b% to cut whipsaw trades. Rebalance daily at the close per the sandbox convention; in practice ~5-10 switches/yr.
params: b = 1.0 (hysteresis band, % beyond SMA required to switch)
candidates.evidence: Gayed & Bilello 2016 Dow Award paper (SSRN 2741701): 2x-above-200dma variant compounds an order of magnitude above buy-and-hold 1928-2015 with ~5 trades/yr; replicated by CXO Advisory (SMA-rule leverage study) and setup4alpha RealTest replication. All design choices are decades old — nothing tuned on post-2025-07 data.
candidates.why_18pct: Arithmetic: with SPX up ~10% and above trend most of the year, 2x close-to-close compounding gives ~19-21% before drag; vol drag at 15% vol is ~2.2%/yr at 2x; costs ~10 switches x 2bps x 200% notional ≈ 0.04% — negligible. So it clears 18% net iff the market has a normal up year and the filter isn't whipsawed. It FAILS in flat/chop years (2015, 2018-like: expect 0-8%) and merely loses less in bear years. This is the highest-probability clear in the whole list, but be honest: it is levered beta with a crash filter, and its 12-month outcome is mostly one draw of the market. Probability of clearing +18% in a random year: roughly 45-55%, versus ~30% for unlevered SPY.

candidates.name: VIX-Spike Panic-Reversion Overlay on Trend-Gated SPY
candidates.mechanism: When spot VIX spikes far above its recent baseline and then rolls over, forced de-risking (vol-target funds, margin liquidations, gamma hedging) has already pushed prices below fair value; liquidity providers earn a premium for catching the recovery. Nagel (2012) shows reversal/liquidity-provision profits concentrate exactly when VIX is high. The counterparty is the forced seller; the edge persists because catching falling knives is career risk for institutions and psychologically hard for retail.
candidates.construction: Baseline book: candidate 1 (trend-gated SPY at 1.5x). Overlay using ^VIX (signal only): let R = VIX / median(VIX, 63d). Panic-entry: if VIX crossed above 30 (or R > 1.8) within the last 5 days AND VIX < VIX_max(5d) x 0.9 (spike rolling over), set SPY target to 2.0x for h=10 trading days. Escalation guard: if VIX > 30 and still making 5-day highs, cut baseline to 0.5x. All rules on daily closes; overlay fires ~2-6 times/yr.
params: vix_spike = 30 (absolute VIX trigger; alt ratio 1.8x of 63d median)
rolloff = 0.9 (VIX must be below 90% of its 5d max to confirm the spike is fading)
candidates.evidence: iPresage VIX mean-reversion backtest: VIX>30 cross-and-recede entries, 68.3% win rate over 187 signals, avg 5-day SPY return +1.84% (~3x unconditional); VIX>50 historically marks bottoms not tops. Nagel 2012 'Evaporating Liquidity' (RFS) for the mechanism. All thresholds are round numbers from pre-2025 literature.
candidates.why_18pct: Baseline 1.5x trend-gated SPY ≈ 13-16% in a normal up year. Overlay: ~4 episodes/yr x ~10 days x (+1.8% conditional 5-day edge extended to ~2.5-3% per 10d episode) x 0.5x incremental exposure ≈ +3-5%/yr, and the escalation guard clips left tail. Costs: a handful of full-notional switches, ~0.1%/yr at ETF rates. Net expectation in an up year: 17-21%. Honest caveat: episode count is small (2-6/yr), so a single failed catch (VIX keeps climbing, e.g. Aug-2015 or Feb-2020 first leg) can erase the year's overlay contribution; the rolloff condition is what backtests say separates the 68%-win entries from knife-catching, but N is small. Credible clear, not a lock.

candidates.name: Vol-Scaled Concentrated 12-1 Momentum (S&P500, long-only, levered)
candidates.mechanism: Underreaction plus slow institutional flow chasing keeps 12-1 winners drifting; the long leg of momentum in mega/large caps carries most of the implementable premium. Momentum's known failure mode (crash when vol explodes after a bear market) is exactly the state vol-scaling de-levers — Cederburg et al. show momentum is the ONE family where vol-management robustly survives out of sample. Counterparty: anchored fundamental investors selling winners too early and averaging into losers.
candidates.construction: Universe: point-in-time S&P500 members. Score: return from t-252 to t-21 (12-1 momentum, closes). Hold top K=20 names, inverse-62d-vol weighted, refreshed monthly in 4 weekly tranches (quarter of the book rotates each week) to cut turnover and timing luck. Portfolio-level scaling a la Barroso-Santa-Clara: target 20% annualized vol using realized 62d portfolio vol, leverage capped at 2.0x, floored at 0.5x. Also hard de-lever to 1.0x when SPY < 200d SMA (momentum crashes are post-bear events).
candidates.evidence: Jegadeesh-Titman 12-1 momentum; Barroso & Santa-Clara 2015 (vol-managed momentum, JFE) and Daniel & Moskowitz 2016 (momentum crashes); Moreira & Muir 2017 vol-managed portfolios; Cederburg et al. 2020 (JFE) confirming momentum is where vol-scaling survives OOS. All parameters are textbook defaults, nothing fit to the holdout year.
candidates.why_18pct: Arithmetic: large-cap long-leg momentum tilt ≈ market + 3-6%/yr historically; at avg ~1.5x scaled leverage: (9% + 4.5%) x 1.5 ≈ 20% gross. Turnover: top-20 12-1 book turns ~150-250%/side/yr; at 10bps/side ≈ 0.3-0.5%/yr — fine. Vol drag at 20% target ≈ 2%. Net ≈ 17-19% in a year where momentum's long leg behaves. Honest caveats: 20-name concentration means huge idiosyncratic variance around that mean (realized could be +35% or +2% in the same regime); the momentum premium itself has had multi-year dry spells; and the 3-6% tilt figure is a long-run average, not a floor. Plausible clear, meaningfully riskier than candidates 1-2.

candidates.name: Sector-ETF Dual Momentum with Trend Gate at 1.5x
candidates.mechanism: Industry momentum (Moskowitz-Grinblatt): sector-level fund flows and analyst herding propagate slowly, so past 3-12m sector leaders keep leading. Doing it in ETFs at 2bps makes the well-known decay of the premium survivable. The absolute-momentum (dual) gate switches to defensive assets in downtrends, letting the book carry leverage safely. Counterparty: benchmark-constrained allocators who rebalance back toward sector weights mechanically.
candidates.construction: Universe: the ~11 SPDR sector ETFs in the panel (+ GLD/TLT as defensive legs if present). Monthly at month-end close: rank sectors by blended (3m + 6m + 12m)/3 total return; hold top 3 equal-weight at L=1.5x IF SPY close > 200d SMA AND the top sector's 12m return > 0; otherwise 100% defensive sleeve (short-Treasury ETF, unlevered). Daily convention: weights only change on the first close of each month.
candidates.evidence: Moskowitz & Grinblatt 1999 industry momentum; ETF implementations documented profitable after costs at ~5%/yr excess (Quantpedia 'Sector Momentum — Rotational System'; QuantConnect dual-momentum sector rotation thread); Antonacci dual momentum for the absolute-momentum gate. Standard-parameter strategy, decades of pre-2025 documentation.
candidates.why_18pct: Arithmetic: market ~9% + 3-5% sector-momentum excess ≈ 12-14% x 1.5 = 18-21% gross. Costs are the beauty: ~30-50% turnover/month on a 3-ETF book at 2bps ≈ 0.15-0.25%/yr; vol drag at 1.5x ≈ 1%. Net ≈ 17-20% in an up year. Honest caveats: the 5%/yr excess is the optimistic end of the literature — post-publication estimates are nearer 2-3%, which puts net at ~15-17%, i.e. this clears the bar only if either the market or the sector spread has a good year. Rank it a credible-but-thin clear; its virtue is near-zero cost and low blow-up risk, making it the best diversifier next to candidate 3.

candidates.name: Large-Cap Residual Reversal Sleeve, VIX-Conditioned (overlay, not standalone)
candidates.mechanism: Liquidity provision: 5-day losers in large caps are pushed below fair value by institutional order-flow imbalance; the reversal is compensation for absorbing that flow. Stripping the sector move (residual vs sector ETF) removes the momentum/industry component that fights the trade. Profits concentrate when VIX is high because that is when liquidity providers are capital-constrained and the premium is fat (Nagel 2012). Counterparty: the impatient institutional seller paying for immediacy.
candidates.construction: Universe: current S&P500 members. Each day compute 5-day residual return = stock 5d return minus its sector ETF 5d return. Long the bottom decile (~50 names, equal weight, capped 3%/name) with 0.3x of NAV; short the top decile with 0.15x (net long tilt) — or long-only losers at 0.3x if shorting is out of scope. Hold 5 days in overlapping daily tranches (1/5 of sleeve rotates daily) to smooth turnover. ACTIVE ONLY when VIX > its 252d median x 1.2; flat otherwise. Runs on top of a 1.2x trend-gated SPY core; total gross ≤ 2x.
candidates.evidence: de Groot, Huij & Zhou (SSRN 1605049): reversal survives costs ONLY in large caps with turnover discipline, 30-50bps/week net in that configuration; Blitz-Huij-Lansdorp-Verbeek residual reversal (doubled Sharpe vs raw reversal); Nagel 2012 for the VIX conditioning; Quantpedia short-term-reversal writeup. Khandani-Lo 2007/2011 for the strategy family's provenance.
candidates.why_18pct: Standalone it does NOT clear 18% — say so plainly. Sleeve arithmetic: 0.45x gross sleeve x optimistic 8-12%/yr gross reversal alpha in large caps ≈ +3.6-5.4% gross; turnover is brutal (sleeve turns ~50x/yr) → costs ≈ 0.45 x 50 x 2 x 10bps ≈ 4.5%/yr... which eats most of it UNLESS the VIX gate keeps it off ~60-70% of the time, cutting cost to ~1.5% while keeping the high-premium episodes: net contribution ≈ +1.5-3%/yr on top of the ~13-15% core. Total ≈ 15-18%. Verdict: STRETCH. Include only as an overlay lifting a core from just-below to at-the-bar; the post-2010 decay of raw reversal is real, and if the fat-VIX episodes don't come, it adds ~0.

candidates.name: Price/Volume Event-Drift (PEAD proxy from gaps), long-only
candidates.mechanism: Post-event drift: investors underreact to large information shocks; PEAD is the classic case but earnings dates aren't in the panel. Proxy: an earnings-like event is a day with |open-gap| > 4% AND volume > 3x its 63d average — this catches most earnings surprises plus guidance/news shocks using only open/close/volume. Upward events drift further up for weeks at small size because institutions build positions slowly against anchored sellers. Persists because the per-name capacity is tiny — irrelevant at our size, fatal at fund scale, which is why it's left on the table.
candidates.construction: Universe: S&P500 members. Event: today's open/yesterday's close - 1 > g% AND today's volume > v x ADV(63d) AND close >= open (gap held, not faded). Enter at today's close, equal-weight all active events, hold H=15 trading days, max 25 concurrent names (skip new events when full), position cap 4%. Sleeve sized 0.5x NAV over a 1.2x trend-gated SPY core (total ≤ 2x). No shorting of down-gaps (borrow/crowding makes the short side unreliable).
candidates.evidence: PEAD: Bernard & Thomas 1989/1990, still-documented-post-costs at small size in large caps (Quantpedia PEAD family); gap-continuation/volume-confirmation studies in the practitioner literature (Quantocracy sphere; QuantConnect PEAD implementations use exactly this style of price-reaction proxy when fundamental dates are absent). The gap+volume event definition is a standard free-data stand-in, not fitted to the holdout year.
candidates.why_18pct: Standalone: no. Sleeve arithmetic: ~150-250 qualifying events/yr in the S&P500; historical post-event 15-day drift on up-gaps-that-hold ≈ +0.5-1.5% over market; with 0.5x average deployment that's +1.5-4%/yr gross; turnover ≈ 0.5 x 17 cycles x 2 x 10bps ≈ 1.7%/yr → net +0.5-2.5% over the ~13-15% core → total ~14-17.5%, i.e. it likely FALLS SHORT of 18% on its own math and is the weakest clear here. STRETCH — included because it is the only genuinely cross-sectional, event-driven, capacity-protected edge constructible from this panel, it is nearly uncorrelated with the trend/vol candidates, and modest parameter luck (a strong earnings-drift year) puts the combined book over the bar.

lit.summary:
## Literature scan: published strategies with a credible path to +18%/yr net on free daily data

**Governing reality check (McLean & Pontiff, JF 2016):** across 97 published cross-sectional predictors, returns fall ~26% out-of-sample and ~58% post-publication. So any published number should be haircut roughly in half before doing the leverage arithmetic. Anomalies that survived best post-publication: time-series trend/momentum applied to indices (Moskowitz-Ooi-Pedersen 2012, JFE; strategy is old and still run at scale by CTAs), the volatility risk premium (structural insurance premium, persists because the loss is a genuine tail; Quantpedia VRP composite ~16%/yr Sharpe ~1 over 17 yrs), and cross-sectional stock momentum (halved but positive; crash risk is the cost of admission — Barroso & Santa-Clara 2015 show vol-managing momentum roughly doubles its Sharpe). Anomalies that decayed worst: PEAD in large caps (essentially arbitraged in S&P 500 names, and unimplementable here anyway — no earnings dates in the panel), classic 4-day turn-of-the-month (Lakonishok-Smidt 1988; recent re-examinations find the [0,+3] window indistinguishable from other days in the last decade), and BAB as published (Novy-Marx & Velikov 2021 show the headline Sharpe 0.78 depends on non-tradable micro-cap weighting; market-neutral BAB in S&P 500 names cannot approach 18% even at 2x).

**Structural rejections under this sandbox:** (1) Overnight anomaly (Cliff-Cooper-Gulen 2008; Boyarchenko-Larsen-Whelan NY Fed 2023) — the entire equity premium accrues close-to-open, but execution here is close-to-close target weights, so the anomaly is literally not harvestable; rejected on feasibility, not evidence. (2) Turn-of-month — even granting the older 5-12bp/day edge in a 7-day window, being in market ~30% of days at 2x yields ~8-10%/yr; fails the bar. (3) Any unlevered market-neutral construct — Sharpe ~0.7-1.0 at ~6-8% vol is ~5-8%/yr; 2x gross gets ~10-14% before costs; fails.

**What clears the bar honestly:** the only designs that reach +18% net with ≤2x leverage are (a) *levered conditional beta* — Gayed & Bilello's 2016 Dow Award "Leverage for the Long Run": 2x S&P above the 200-day SMA, T-bills below; ~5 rotations/yr so ETF costs are negligible; the filter exists precisely because leverage + high-vol regimes is what kills 2x buy-and-hold; (b) *short-vol carry with a level/RV kill switch* — SVXY is -0.5x post-Volmageddon (a repeat of Feb-2018 costs ~-48%, not -96%), and the VRP (VIX minus subsequent realized vol) is positive ~85% of months; (c) *levered dual momentum* — Antonacci's GEM printed 17.4%/yr 1974-2013 with 0.87 Sharpe, decayed roughly per McLean-Pontiff post-2013, so it needs 1.5-2x on the equity leg to clear 18%; (d) *vol-managed levered momentum stocks* — long-only top-decile 12-1 momentum within the S&P 500 at ~1.5x with a trend/vol filter. All four are long-biased and their 18% arithmetic is conditional on equities not having a flat-chop year; that is the honest price of the target. Candidates ranked by credibility of the arithmetic below; (b) and (d) are the biggest stretches.

Sources: [MOP 2012 JFE](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0304405X11002613) · [McLean & Pontiff 2016](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/jofi.12365) · [Gayed & Bilello 2016](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2741701) · [Antonacci GEM extended backtest](https://www.optimalmomentum.com/extended-backtest-of-global-equities-momentum/) · [Quantpedia VRP](https://quantpedia.com/strategies/volatility-risk-premium-effect) · [CFA Institute Volmageddon post-mortem](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/financial-analysts-journal/2021/volmageddon-failure-short-volatility-products) · [Frazzini & Pedersen 2014](https://pages.stern.nyu.edu/~lpederse/papers/BettingAgainstBeta.pdf) · [Novy-Marx & Velikov critique](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0304405X21002051) · [Quantseeker TOM re-examination](https://www.quantseeker.com/p/turn-of-the-month-strategies-do-they) · [NY Fed Overnight Drift](https://www.newyorkfed.org/medialibrary/media/research/staff_reports/sr917.pdf)

candidates.name: Leveraged Trend Rotation (Gayed-Bilello LRS, 2x)
candidates.mechanism: Volatility clusters, and the worst drawdowns occur below long-term trend; above trend, realized vol is lower and returns are higher, so leverage is applied only in the regime where the variance drag of daily 2x exposure is small and crash risk is lowest. The other side is every investor whose mandate, margin constraints, or loss aversion forces deleveraging after drawdowns begin — the edge persists because it is a risk-transfer premium (bearing equity beta in calm regimes, handing it back in storms), not a mispricing that arbitrage removes. Gayed & Bilello won the 2016 Charles H. Dow Award with this exact construction, and it has been fully public for a decade without the 200DMA signal degrading, consistent with time-series trend being one of the few published effects that survived post-publication (MOP 2012 lineage).
candidates.construction: Daily at the close: if SPY adjusted close > 200-day SMA of SPY adjusted close, target weight = 2.0x SPY; else target weight = 1.0x SHY (or BIL if in the ETF list; cash if neither). Optional buffer: require close to cross the SMA by ±1% before switching, to cut whipsaw. All signals from adjusted close only; ~4-8 rebalances/yr at 2 bps/side ETF cost ≈ 2-3 bps/yr total drag.
candidates.evidence: Gayed & Bilello, 'Leverage for the Long Run' (SSRN 2741701, 2016 Dow Award): 1928-2015, 2x LRS turned $10k into $39B vs $19M for buy-and-hold; outperforms constant leverage in every subperiod tested. Moskowitz-Ooi-Pedersen (JFE 2012) document the underlying 12-month trend effect across 58 instruments, 1985-2009. CXO Advisory replications confirm SMA-gated leverage beats constant leverage on risk-adjusted basis. Trend filters are among the best post-publication survivors (still the core of the CTA industry).
candidates.why_18pct: SPY spends ~70-75% of days above its 200DMA, and conditional on being above, annualized return is ~14-16% at ~13% vol. 2x that, minus daily-reset variance drag (~σ² ≈ 1.7%/yr at 13% vol), minus near-zero costs ≈ 24-27% in an up-trending year; T-bill yield (~4-5%) accrues when flat. Honest failure mode: a flat, choppy year (2015, 2018) whipsaws to roughly -5% to +5% and misses badly; a year that starts below trend and rips (2023-style V) also lags. Roughly 55-60% of historical 12-month windows clear +18%. This is conditional 2x beta with a crash filter — the most credible candidate on the list, but it is a market bet, not alpha.

candidates.name: Levered Dual Momentum ETF Rotation (Antonacci GEM adapted)
candidates.mechanism: Combines cross-sectional (relative) momentum across asset-class ETFs with absolute momentum as a regime gate. The counterparties are benchmark-anchored allocators who rebalance against trends and under-react to regime shifts over 3-12 month horizons; the effect persists post-publication because exploiting it requires tolerating multi-quarter tracking error to the S&P, which institutions structurally cannot do. Absolute momentum (12-month excess return > 0) is the same time-series momentum effect as MOP 2012.
candidates.construction: Monthly at last close: compute trailing 252-day total return for SPY, QQQ, and an intl/defensive sleeve from the ETF list (e.g. EFA or EEM if present, else skip). If the best equity ETF's 12-month return > 12-month return of SHY (absolute-momentum gate), hold that single equity ETF at 1.5x. Else hold TLT or AGG (whichever is in the list) at 1.0x. One position, ≤12 switches/yr, 2 bps/side: ≤5 bps/yr cost. All from adjusted close.
candidates.evidence: Antonacci, 'Dual Momentum Investing' (2014) / Optimal Momentum extended backtest: GEM 17.43%/yr, 0.87 Sharpe, -22.7% maxDD over 1974-2013; extended 1950-2018 sample includes two out-of-sample periods that held up. Post-publication live decay is real (GEM lagged SPY most of 2015-2020, whipsawed in 2020) — consistent with McLean-Pontiff halving. Robot Wealth and Invest Resolve replications confirm the effect but flag single-parameter (lookback) fragility.
candidates.why_18pct: McLean-Pontiff haircut on 17.4% gives ~9-12% unlevered expectation. 1.5x on the equity leg lifts a QQQ-dominant regime (which trailing data through 2025-07 would select) to ~1.5 × 13-15% ≈ 20-22% in a continued-trend year, with the absolute-momentum gate capping left tail near -15%. Honest assessment: this is a STRETCH — it clears 18% only if the 2015-2025 megacap-tech trend persists through mid-2026, and a single whipsaw (sharp crash + V-recovery inside the 12-month lookback) can cost 10-15% versus buy-and-hold. Call it 45-50% odds of clearing the bar, better odds of landing 10-18%.

candidates.name: VIX-Gated Short-Vol Carry (SVXY + kill switch)
candidates.mechanism: The volatility risk premium: implied vol (VIX) exceeds subsequently realized vol in roughly 85% of months by ~3-4 vol points, because hedgers systematically overpay for crash insurance and the sellers bear a genuine, occasionally realized tail. This is a structural insurance premium, not an anomaly — it survived full publication, an entire product ecosystem, and the Feb-2018 Volmageddon that killed XIV; it persists because the 2018-style left tail is real and most capital is not allowed to hold it. SVXY now runs -0.5x VIX short-term futures, so a repeat of the worst single day in the product's history costs ~-48%, not termination.
candidates.construction: Daily at the close, compute (a) VIX close, (b) 10-day realized vol of SPY close-to-close returns (annualized). Risk-ON when VIX < vix_ceiling AND VIX > rv_mult × realized (premium exists and level is calm): hold 50% SVXY + 50% SPY. Risk-OFF (either condition fails): 100% SHY/cash. VIX used as signal only; SVXY and SPY are the tradables. Expect ~10-25 switches/yr; 2 bps/side on ETFs keeps costs <15 bps/yr.
candidates.evidence: Quantpedia 'Volatility Risk Premium Effect': systematic VRP capture ~16.3%/yr, Sharpe ~1.0 over 17 years. Academic term-structure timing (e.g. Simon & Campasano 2014-lineage work; Quantpedia 'Exploiting Term Structure of VIX Futures'): exiting when the curve inverts preserves return while cutting tail risk (~3.4%/mo four-factor alpha in the cited study). CFA Institute FAJ (2021) Volmageddon post-mortem documents both the -90%+ one-day loss mechanism and SVXY's subsequent deleveraging to -0.5x. Post-2018 the premium re-fattened: fewer sellers.
candidates.why_18pct: In a calm contango year SVXY at -0.5x returns +30% to +70% (2023-24 realized this); 50% SVXY + 50% SPY in a calm year ≈ 0.5×45% + 0.5×12% ≈ +28%, and the VIX>25 switch caps a vol-event year near -10% to -20% rather than -50%. Honest assessment: STRETCH with a fat left tail. The 12 months ending 2026-07 clear +18% only if no sustained VIX>25 regime occurs; a single gap event (overnight vol spike straight through the daily-close filter — the filter cannot act intraday) takes -15% to -25% in days. Probability of clearing the bar ~40-45%; expected value is decent but the distribution is brutally skewed. Sized at 50% precisely because full-weight SVXY is uninsurable at daily granularity.

candidates.name: Vol-Filtered Concentrated Momentum (long-only 12-1, 1.5x, trend-gated)
candidates.mechanism: Cross-sectional momentum: investors under-react to firm-specific trends (anchoring, disposition effect) and momentum's crash risk deters arbitrage capital, which is why it decayed less than most anomalies post-publication. The known killer — momentum crashes in post-panic rebounds (2009, 2020) — is largely forecastable from market trend and volatility (Barroso & Santa-Clara 2015; Daniel & Moskowitz 2016), so gating the leverage on a market-level trend/vol filter targets exactly the failure mode. Long-only implementation avoids the short leg, which is where crash losses and borrow costs live.
candidates.construction: Monthly at last close: within current S&P 500 members (point-in-time mask), rank by 231-day return skipping the last 21 days (12-1). Long the top 50 names equal-weight. Apply gross leverage 1.5x when SPY > its 200-day SMA AND 20-day realized SPY vol < 25%; drop to 1.0x when one condition fails; go 100% SHY/cash when SPY < 200DMA. Monthly turnover ~15-25% of names; at 10 bps/side stocks ≈ 60-120 bps/yr cost drag.
params: leverage schedule = {1.5x trend+lowvol, 1.0x mixed, 0 below trend}
candidates.evidence: Jegadeesh & Titman (1993) original; McLean-Pontiff (2016) show momentum among the persistent post-publication survivors (halved, not dead). Barroso & Santa-Clara (JFE 2015): scaling momentum by realized vol roughly doubles its Sharpe and cuts crash risk an order of magnitude. Daniel & Moskowitz (JFE 2016): momentum crashes are forecastable by bear-market state + high vol — exactly the gate used here. AQR's live large-cap momentum funds confirm the tilt survives implementation costs in liquid names.
candidates.why_18pct: Arithmetic: large-cap long-only momentum tilt earns market +3-5% gross historically; haircut post-publication to market +2-3%. Market ~10-12% + 2.5% tilt = ~13-14% unlevered, ×1.5 = ~20%, minus ~1% costs and ~1% variance drag ≈ 18-19%. Honest assessment: STRETCH — every term in that product must come in near the top of its range, and the tilt estimate is the softest number (post-2018 large-cap momentum has been feast-or-famine, though the trailing-2025 AI regime was feast). Concentration in 50 names adds idiosyncratic dispersion of ±4%/yr around the estimate. ~40-45% odds of clearing 18%; the trend gate makes the failure years survivable (-5% to +5%) rather than catastrophic.

candidates.name: Volatility-Managed Market (Moreira-Muir, capped 2x)
candidates.mechanism: Realized variance is highly persistent but expected returns are not proportionally higher after vol spikes, so the realized Sharpe of equity exposure is strongly time-varying and inversely related to trailing variance. Scaling exposure by inverse variance harvests this: less exposure exactly when the risk-return trade-off is worst. The other side is the vast pool of constant-weight capital (target-date funds, 60/40 mandates) that cannot vary exposure; the effect persists because acting on it requires both leverage authority in calm markets and the discipline to deleverage into panics — institutionally rare.
candidates.construction: Daily at the close: w_t = min(2.0, c / RV²) where RV = 21-day realized vol of SPY close-to-close returns (annualized) and c is calibrated so the full-sample average weight ≈ 1.2-1.3 (e.g. c = 0.14²·1.25 with pre-2025 data only). Hold w_t × SPY, remainder in SHY. Smooth w_t with a 5-day average and only trade when target weight moves >10 percentage points, keeping turnover ~15-25x/yr at 2 bps/side ≈ 6-10 bps/yr.
candidates.evidence: Moreira & Muir, 'Volatility-Managed Portfolios' (Journal of Finance 2017): vol-managed market factor earns ~4.9%/yr alpha over buy-and-hold and raises Sharpe ~25%; effect strongest for the market factor and momentum. Subsequent debate (Cederburg et al. 2020) shows the alpha shrinks out-of-sample and depends on leverage in calm regimes actually being available — which this sandbox grants (≤2x). Related lineage: risk-parity / vol-targeting is standard industry practice, i.e. survived publication at scale.
candidates.why_18pct: Average weight ~1.25-1.4 on a ~10-12% market gives ~13-16%, plus 1-3% timing alpha (haircut from Moreira-Muir's 4.9% per Cederburg critique), plus T-bill carry on the residual ≈ 15-19%. Honest assessment: the WEAKEST candidate against an 18% bar — it reliably beats buy-and-hold risk-adjusted but only clears +18% absolute when the market itself does ~12%+ in a low-vol grind (which, notably, is what a calm continuation of the pre-2025-07 regime looks like). ~35-40% odds. Included because it is the best-pedigreed diversifier to pair with candidate 1 or 4, not as a standalone bar-clearer.

inhouse.summary:
## In-house synthesis — what the lab's own record says (all evidence from ~/projects/alpha-lab, data ≤ 2025-07-10)

**What died and exactly why (memos/diagnostics-2026-07-10.md, tracks/statarb/STATE.md):**
- Daily residual reversal on S&P 500 large caps is DEAD: implementable gross ~1.3-1.6%/yr (Sharpe ~0.3, 63% win rate) vs 5.34%/yr turnover cost at 5.3%/day one-way turnover. The kill was cost-per-unit-of-turnover, NOT absence of information. The 63% win rate is real.
- The kappa screen died by INVERSION: slow-half-life entries earned MORE per trade (Q5 slow: 0.0195 mean P&L, 73.9% win vs Q1 fast: 0.0104, 70.4%). Implication: the paying information is slow — it does not need daily rebalancing to harvest, which is exactly what the cost math demands.
- Earnings blackout REJECTED (memos/earnings-blackout-verdict.md): entries around earnings were the BEST trades (+12.4 bps/pos-day pre-earnings, +6.1 blocked vs +0.9 average). Post-event dislocations are where the reversion/drift premium concentrates.
- ~43% of the old book's return sat in deep dips (s < −1.75), the −2σ to −3σ band, which also carried the tail risk (survivorship caveat noted).

**What survived:**
- PEAD: +8.18-8.45% 60d top-minus-bottom SUE CAR, monotone across horizons, 530 events (artifacts/pead/drift.md). Survivorship-biased 60-name universe, gross, no portfolio yet — but it is the only positive-alpha event result in the lab, and it is LOW-frequency (60d holds ≈ 1.7%/day turnover, ~0.9%/yr cost vs statarb's 5.3%).
- The equal-weight universe benchmark: Sharpe 1.07 (momentum PIT run), 1.00 (low-vol, value), 1.35 (asset-growth), 2.10 gross for equal-weight-all-anomalies (GKX). Every clever L/S the lab built lost to naive equal-weight long. GKX Stage-4 verdict: timing/rotation adds negative value vs diversified holding.
- Momentum L/S 0.08, low-vol −0.35, value −0.05 on PIT S&P 500: cross-sectional factor L/S inside the 500 is empty at this breadth. Do not propose market-neutral factor books.

**Design logic that falls out:**
1. The 18% bar is unreachable market-neutral (the lab proved its best-audited neutral book grosses ~1.5%). Long-biased levered beta is the chassis; alpha sleeves are kickers.
2. Where costs killed reversal, the fix is not a better signal — it is 10-30x less turnover on the same information: deep-only entries, long horizon, index-level regime expression (ETF at 2 bps), event-driven holds.
3. Where the blackout memo and PEAD agree (post-event dislocations pay), express PEAD with price+volume only: SUE is not in the sandbox fields, but announcement days are detectable as gap+volume shocks, and drift direction = shock sign.
4. Financing note applied honestly throughout: the sandbox charges only trading costs, so 2x arithmetic is used as specified; if real margin financing (~4-5% on borrowed notional) were charged, every levered candidate's bar rises by that amount.

**Ranked honesty on clearing +18% net:** Candidate 2 (levered EW core) and 5 (composite book) clear it whenever the EW S&P year is ≥ ~10-11% — engineered beta, no alpha pretense. Candidate 1 (gap-drift) is the only real-alpha claim, backed by the lab's own +8% drift measurement, and needs the market roughly flat-or-up. Candidates 3 and 4 are stretches and labeled as such. All parameters are long-history defaults, none tuned on post-2025-07 data. Trial accounting: 5 candidates × ~2 params each ≈ 10-12 effective trials for any deflated-Sharpe claim downstream.

candidates.name: Gap-Drift: price/volume PEAD proxy, concentrated long, 1.5x
candidates.mechanism: Post-earnings-announcement drift is underreaction by investors who anchor on pre-announcement expectations and update slowly; the other side is the anchoring crowd plus index/passive flow that ignores firm-level news. The lab's own event study measured +8.18-8.45% 60d top-minus-bottom CAR on 530 events, monotone in horizon, and the earnings-blackout memo independently found entries around announcements were the book's best trades (+12.4 bps/pos-day). The edge persists because harvesting it requires holding single-name event risk for 60 days, which fast money will not warehouse and slow money does not screen for.
candidates.construction: Universe = PIT S&P 500 members. Shock day t for name i: z_i = 1d close-to-close return / 60d rolling std, and volume_t ≥ 3× trailing 20d median volume. Positive event if z ≥ +2.5 with the volume confirm (gap+volume is the free-data announcement detector; no fundamentals needed). Enter at close t+1 (skip-1 house discipline), equal-weight all active events, hold 60 trading days, 5% per-name cap, ~20-40 names live at any time; idle capital parked in SPY. Scale book to 1.5x gross. Rebalance only on entry/exit — no daily re-equalization (kills the churn that killed statarb).
params: hold = 60 trading days (matches the measured drift horizon)
candidates.evidence: artifacts/pead/drift.md (+8.18% 60d CAR spread, 530 events); tracks/pead/STATE.md (+8.45%, monotone 5/20/60d); memos/earnings-blackout-verdict.md (event-window entries were the best trades in the statarb log, n=8,038 position-days); diagnostics-2026-07-10.md turnover section (cost kill is linear in turnover — this design runs ~1.7%/day one-way vs statarb's 5.3%).
candidates.why_18pct: Arithmetic: long-leg abnormal drift ≈ half the 8.2% spread ≈ +4%/60d, haircut for survivorship and the top-quintile-to-z-threshold translation to +2-3%/60d; a continuously-full event book annualizes that to roughly +6-10% over market. Beta ≈ 1.0 on event names. At 1.5x: 1.5×(10% market + 5% drift, mid-haircut) ≈ 22.5% gross, minus costs ~1.3%/yr (turnover ~1.7%/day one-way at 10 bps/side plus SPY parking at 2 bps), minus concentration vol drag ~1-2% → ~19-20%. HONEST: the drift number is gross, survivorship-flattered, from 60 names; the gap+volume proxy will catch non-earnings shocks whose drift is weaker; and the whole thing needs the market roughly flat-or-up (beta 1.5 dominates in a down year). This is the only candidate whose excess-over-beta claim is backed by an in-house measurement — call it a defensible stretch.

candidates.name: Levered Equal-Weight Core with VIX tail gate (2x EW-500)
candidates.mechanism: Not an anomaly claim — an engineering claim built on the lab's most replicated finding: the equal-weight S&P 500 benchmark posted Sharpe 1.0-1.35 in every PIT run (momentum, low-vol, value, asset-growth scorecards) and beat every L/S strategy the lab constructed, and GKX's Stage-4 verdict was that timing loses to diversified holding. The other side is nobody: it is the equity risk premium plus the small EW-over-cap-weight rebalancing/size tilt, levered. The VIX gate exists only to cut the left tail that makes 2x daily-rebalanced leverage path-dependent.
candidates.construction: Hold every current S&P 500 member (PIT mask) equal-weight. Rebalance a name only when its weight drifts more than 20% relative from target, plus membership adds/drops (Garleanu-Pedersen no-trade band per the roadmap — the band, not daily re-equalization, keeps stock turnover near index-fund levels). Leverage L_t set at each close from ^VIX (signal only): L = 2.0 while VIX < 30; L = 1.0 while VIX ≥ 30; re-enter 2.0 after 5 consecutive closes back under 30 (hysteresis kills whipsaw churn). Thresholds are long-history round numbers, not fitted.
params: band = 20% relative weight drift before trading a name
candidates.evidence: artifacts/momentum/scorecard_pit.md (equal_weight_universe Sharpe 1.07), artifacts/low_vol + value scorecard_pit.md (1.00 each), tracks/asset_growth/STATE.md (1.35); tracks/gkx/STATE.md (equal-weight-all-signals 2.10 vs rotation 0.78 — diversification beats timing); alpha-roadmap item E (no-trade bands: 25-50% turnover cut for <5% alpha give-up).
candidates.why_18pct: 2×μ − k(k−1)σ²/2 drag: with EW arithmetic return ~12% and vol ~17%, geometric ≈ 2×12% − 5.8% + unlevered-drag credit ≈ 18-19%; costs are trivial (banded EW turnover ~5%/mo one-way at 10 bps ≈ 0.12%/yr; gate trades a handful of times/yr). Clears 18% if and only if the EW S&P year is ≥ ~10-11% arithmetic — which its own sample delivered most years, but a flat/down year fails outright and no alpha is claimed. Also honest: the sandbox charges no financing; real margin at ~4-5% on the borrowed notional would raise the required EW year to ~14%. This is the highest-probability candidate precisely because it pretends to nothing.

candidates.name: Deep-Dip Slow-Reversion, long-only concentrated (the low-turnover inversion of the statarb kill)
candidates.mechanism: The statarb kill was arithmetic, not informational: 63% win rate and positive gross survived the engine fix, and two in-house findings point at where the information density is highest — slow-half-life entries earned nearly 2x fast ones per trade (killed kappa screen's inverted result), and ~43% of the old book's return lived in the −2σ to −3σ deep-dip band. The other side is forced/discretionary sellers completing liquidations in large caps; the edge persists at deep thresholds because deep dips are rare enough that harvesting them cannot support the turnover-heavy industry version. Long-only expression converts the unhedgeable drift-drag term (which killed the neutral book) into mostly market beta plus a reversion kicker.
candidates.construction: PIT S&P 500 members. Rolling 60d OLS of each stock's daily return on its sector ETF (lagged beta, existing engine code path); s-score on the 60d cumulative residual. LONG-only entry at close t+1 when s ≤ −2.5 (vs the dead book's −1.25); exit when s ≥ −0.5 or after 25 trading days, whichever first. Equal-weight up to 15 concurrent names; capital not deployed in dips sits in SPY. 1.5x gross on the whole book. No shorts (borrow, squeeze tail), no daily re-equalization.
candidates.evidence: diagnostics-2026-07-10.md half-life table (slow Q5: 0.0195/trade, 73.9% win — do NOT screen out slow names); tracks/statarb/STATE.md falling-knife decomposition (deep-dip band = 43% of return); turnover decomposition (5.3%/day killed it; deep-only entries are ~5-8x rarer → sleeve turnover ~1.5%/day one-way ≈ 0.8%/yr cost, a 6x cost reduction on the same information).
candidates.why_18pct: STRETCH, flagged as such. The honest implementable gross of the FULL neutral book was only ~1.3-1.6%/yr; the claim here is that concentrating in the deep band (higher per-trade P&L, the lab's own tables) and dropping the hedge (keep beta) changes the arithmetic: effective beta ~1.2 (dip names are high-beta) × 1.5x ≈ 1.8 × 10% market = 18% beta contribution, + deep-dip reversion kicker maybe +2-4% (survivor-flattered, the falling-knife caveat cuts exactly here), − 0.8% costs → 19-21% in an up year. But the deep-dip band is also where delisting survivorship bias is worst and where a down-market year concentrates losses at 1.8 effective beta. Mostly levered beta wearing a reversion hat; the kicker is real but unprovable on free data (the lab said so itself).

candidates.name: VIX-Spike Panic Buyer (index-level reversal at ETF costs)
candidates.mechanism: The dispersion/volatility-conditioning literature the roadmap vetted (Dai-Medhat-Novy-Marx-Rizova; Hameed-Mian; Nagel) says liquidity-provision returns concentrate after volatility spikes — the same information the dead statarb book harvested cross-sectionally, expressed at the index level where turnover is ~zero and costs are 2 bps. The other side is levered and risk-managed players forced to de-gross into spikes. It persists because providing liquidity into a panic requires pre-committed dry powder and tolerance for early drawdown.
candidates.construction: Base position 1.5x SPY, held continuously. Spike trigger at the close: VIX ≥ 1.5× its own trailing 60d median (relative spike — regime-proof, unlike a fixed level). While triggered, raise to 2.0x SPY; release back to 1.5x when VIX < 1.1× its 60d median. All in SPY at 2 bps/side; a full spike cycle costs ~2 bps round-trip on the 0.5x add. Two to five triggers per year historically.
candidates.evidence: alpha-roadmap-2026-07.md items F and [regime] VIX-scaled gross (vetted citations: NBER w30917, Hameed-Mian JFQA 2015, Nagel — reversal pays after vol spikes); diagnostics turnover lesson (the SAME conditioning at stock level costs 5.3%/yr; at index level ~0.01%/yr); statarb kill memo (cross-sectional daily expression dead, regime expression untested and cost-free).
candidates.why_18pct: STRETCH, conditional. Decomposition: base 1.5x on a +10-12% SPY year ≈ 15-17% after vol drag; the panic add historically contributes +1-4% in years containing a spike-and-recover episode (buying 0.5x extra into post-spike weeks with elevated forward returns). Clears 18% only in a year that is both decently up AND contains at least one VIX spike cycle; a calm grind-up year lands ~15-17% (fail), a spike-into-prolonged-bear year does worse than base (the 2.0x add eats the continuation). In expectation this hovers at the bar rather than clearing it — included because it is the only cost-free expression of the one information source the lab actually validated (post-dislocation reversion), and it composes cleanly with Candidate 2.

candidates.name: The Book: EW core + gap-drift sleeve + panic add (composite, 2.0x cap)
candidates.mechanism: Portfolio-level synthesis of everything that survived in-house: the EW benchmark that beat every strategy (chassis), the PEAD drift that is the lab's only measured positive alpha (kicker), and post-spike liquidity provision at ETF cost (convexity), with the GKX lesson (do not time, diversify) governing the whole: sleeves are static weights, not rotated. Who is on the other side: passive-flow anchoring (drift sleeve), forced de-grossers (panic sleeve), nobody (core).
candidates.construction: Fixed sleeve weights, rebalanced to sleeve targets monthly: 1.2x equal-weight PIT S&P 500 core (20% no-trade band per name) + 0.5x Gap-Drift sleeve (Candidate 1 rules: z ≥ 2.5, 3x volume, 60d hold) + 0.3x SPY that goes to work only on the Candidate-4 VIX spike trigger (sits in SPY at 0.3x otherwise). Whole-book gate: if VIX ≥ 40 at the close, halve total gross until 5 closes below 30. Gross never exceeds 2.0x.
params: book_gate: VIX 40 halve, re-enter after 5 closes < 30
candidates.evidence: Union of the above: EW Sharpe 1.0-1.35 across four PIT scorecards; PEAD +8.2% 60d drift (artifacts/pead/drift.md); roadmap regime items; GKX anti-timing verdict (static sleeve weights, no rotation); statarb turnover post-mortem (every sleeve here runs <2%/day one-way, total book cost <1.5%/yr).
candidates.why_18pct: Effective beta ≈ 1.9-2.0 in normal regimes → 2×(10-12% market) − ~5% levered vol drag ≈ 15-19% from beta alone, + drift-sleeve alpha 0.5×(4-8% excess) ≈ +2-4%, + panic sleeve 0-1.5% → central estimate 18-23% in an average up year, with the VIX-40 gate trimming the left tail that 2x books die of. Costs ~1.2%/yr all-in (mostly the drift sleeve's stock turnover). Fails in a flat/down year like every long-biased design must; under real financing costs the hurdle rises ~4-5%. Most likely composite to clear the bar because the alpha sleeve is additive rather than load-bearing: the market only has to do ~10-11% for the whole book to print 18%.
